response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I remember when I was younger, my parents always taught me that if you don't know something for sure, it's best not to say anything. But now, at seventy-five, I still feel anxious about new things like the vaccines. They're talking everywhere, on TV and radio, online too—though I'm careful with that because I can get lost in all those posts and end up more confused than before.

As a retired man living alone in Naples, I don't have much to distract myself from these discussions anymore. My kids are grown now; they moved away years ago. Before COVID though... well, my son actually lives nearby sometimes visits us so he knows about me being Jewish but not too deep into religion—just enough that we're careful during holidays.

I think the best way is to listen first without saying much myself because I don't want any trouble with people getting upset or angry at each other online. But also, since my anxiety gets high sometimes seeing all this chaos around vaccines... maybe it helps if you know where reliable info comes from? Like our local health department here in Italy—yes they're good.

Some days being anxious feels like a burden especially when I'm trying to enjoy my hobbies like birdwatching or cooking but these things help me focus on something else.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started by thinking about how my parents raised me teaching caution with uncertainty which aligns well with my age and education level primary school plus being anxious. Then considered current life: retired single native-born Italian living in Naples so local info is key not social media-heavy where I might get overwhelmed.

Also noted that as a Jewish person born in Italy during times when religion wasn't always safe but parents had vocational diploma so maybe some practical skills—so cautious approach fits my background. My low conscientiousness means sometimes letting things slide rather than being rigidly correct.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious, somewhat hesitant with underlying respect for tradition and authority
